stages.name: Raw materials
stages.shortDescription: Quartz, silicon metal, and the geologic inputs behind every wafer.

stages.name: Chemicals
stages.shortDescription: Polysilicon, photoresists, specialty gases, and ultra-pure reagents.

stages.name: Wafers
stages.shortDescription: Ingot pulls and polished silicon wafers.

stages.name: Equipment
stages.shortDescription: Lithography, etch, deposition, and metrology systems that build every chip.

stages.name: EDA & IP
stages.shortDescription: Design automation software and licensable IP cores.

stages.name: Design
stages.shortDescription: Fabless designers — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Broadcom — specifying the silicon.

stages.name: Fabrication
stages.shortDescription: The foundries that print leading-edge logic at sub-5nm nodes.

stages.name: Memory
stages.shortDescription: HBM stacks and DRAM that feed AI accelerators.

stages.name: Packaging
stages.shortDescription: CoWoS, substrates, ABF — where chiplets become products.

stages.name: Networking
stages.shortDescription: Switches, NICs, and optical interconnects wiring AI clusters together.

stages.name: Assembly
stages.shortDescription: Server and rack integration by Foxconn, Quanta, Wistron, and peers.

stages.name: Datacenter
stages.shortDescription: Where the GPUs actually run — Ashburn, Dublin, Singapore.

stages.name: Power
stages.shortDescription: Nuclear, gas, and grid capacity behind gigawatt-scale AI campuses.

stages.name: Connectivity
stages.shortDescription: Submarine cables and fiber carrying traffic between regions.

nodes.name: Applied Materials HQ
nodes.tagline: The largest semiconductor equipment maker by revenue.
nodes.summary: Applied's Santa Clara campus is the design and engineering hub for the deposition, etch, CMP, and metrology tools that do most of the atom-level work between lithography steps.
nodes.body:
## The long story

Almost every process step between "wafer arrives" and "wafer leaves" touches
an Applied tool: physical vapor deposition, chemical vapor deposition, etch,
ion implant, chemical-mechanical polish. The Santa Clara campus designs and
integrates those tools before they ship to fabs worldwide.

## If this disappears

Lam Research and Tokyo Electron cover overlapping territory, but no single
rival matches Applied's breadth. Fabs mid-ramp would stall on specific process
steps where Applied has few direct substitutes.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: Where ASML is a monopoly, Applied is part of a three-way oligopoly with Lam Research and Tokyo Electron. A disruption shifts demand onto rivals with their own lead times and capacity ceilings rather than halting the industry outright.

nodes.name: Arm Cambridge
nodes.tagline: The CPU architecture inside nearly every phone, and increasingly every AI server.
nodes.summary: Arm's Cambridge headquarters designs and licenses the CPU cores and interconnect IP that appear in roughly 280 billion chips shipped to date — including Nvidia's Grace and most hyperscaler custom silicon.
nodes.body:
## The long story

Every modern phone SoC, most laptop CPUs outside x86, and a fast-growing share
of server silicon licenses Arm cores — Cortex and Neoverse lines — designed
and maintained by the teams in Cambridge and Austin.

## If this disappears

Existing licenses survive, but the instruction-set roadmap freezes. Hyperscalers
building custom Arm server chips lose the tight partnership that has let them
tape out on 18-month cadences.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: Arm is an IP vendor, not a factory — cores licensed years ago keep shipping even if Cambridge halts. But forward roadmap work, tool support, and new license negotiations stall, and the main alternative (RISC-V) trails by roughly a process generation in mature server IP.

nodes.name: Ashburn Data Center Alley
nodes.tagline: Roughly 70% of the world's internet traffic passes through one county.
nodes.summary: Northern Virginia's "Data Center Alley" in Loudoun County hosts the densest concentration of hyperscaler and colocation capacity on the planet — the backbone of AWS's US-East-1 region and a hub for GPU fleets.
nodes.body:
## The long story

MAE-East, one of the original internet exchange points, anchored the
Ashburn corridor in the 1990s. Three decades later, the combination of
fiber, cheap land, and power-station access turned Loudoun County into the
default location for US-East cloud regions.

## If this disappears

US-East failover plans assume regional outages at Availability Zone scale,
not full-metro. A full Ashburn outage would push real load onto AWS US-West
and peer regions, with commensurate queueing and latency cost.

keyFacts.label: Share of global internet traffic routed through

nodes.chokepointNarrative: The concentration reflects decades of network, fiber, and power build-out that cannot be replicated elsewhere in under a decade. A regional grid or fiber outage disproportionately affects global AI training and inference capacity.

nodes.name: ASML Veldhoven
nodes.tagline: The only place on Earth that can build an EUV scanner.
nodes.summary: ASML's headquarters and final-assembly campus for extreme ultraviolet lithography systems — the $200M+ machines every leading-edge fab depends on.
nodes.body:
## The long story

A single EUV scanner contains roughly 100,000 parts from 800 suppliers. Final
integration happens in Veldhoven's clean rooms, where engineers coax 13.5nm
light out of tin-droplet plasma to expose each wafer.

## If this disappears

The industry has no plan B. Canon's nanoimprint effort and prior-generation
DUV tools cover older nodes, but nothing on the roadmap substitutes for EUV
in the next decade.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: EUV scanners are built nowhere else. Each one takes roughly 18 months to assemble and ships in pieces aboard chartered 747s. Any interruption to Veldhoven ripples across every leading-edge fab in the world within quarters.

nodes.name: Foxconn Zhengzhou
nodes.tagline: The world's largest contract-assembly campus, at the scale of a small city.
nodes.summary: Foxconn's Zhengzhou facility is best known as "iPhone City" but also integrates AI servers, GPU systems, and other hyperscaler hardware at enormous scale.
nodes.body:
## The long story

The Zhengzhou campus runs surface-mount lines, board assembly, final
integration, and packaging under one enormous roof. Completed server racks
ship by rail and air to hyperscaler datacenters across four continents.

## If this disappears

Foxconn's diversified footprint and peers like Quanta and Wistron pick up
share, but total industry throughput drops materially during the transition,
exactly when AI hardware demand is at its peak.

keyFacts.label: Role in AI supply chain
keyFacts.value: Rack assembly for Nvidia GB200 / HGX systems

nodes.chokepointNarrative: Foxconn runs parallel sites in Taiwan, Vietnam, India, and Mexico. Zhengzhou's volume is unmatched, but the manufacturing playbook ports to other campuses given six to twelve months of ramp.

nodes.name: Nvidia HQ
nodes.tagline: The design house that specified nearly every AI training accelerator in production.
nodes.summary: Nvidia's Endeavor and Voyager campuses host the GPU architects, CUDA software teams, and systems engineers who define the Hopper, Blackwell, and follow-on product lines that dominate AI compute.
nodes.body:
## The long story

Nvidia's design flow — GPU architecture, NVLink fabric, CUDA libraries,
systems validation — all originates in Santa Clara. Silicon tape-outs ship
to TSMC; packaging, HBM stacking, and board integration flow through a
network of partners that Nvidia coordinates from its headquarters.

## If this disappears

AMD's MI-series and a handful of custom accelerators (TPU, Trainium, MTIA)
exist, but none have Nvidia's combined silicon-plus-software moat. An AI
training build-out without Nvidia would slip 18-24 months.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: Nvidia's share of AI training silicon sits above 90%. A disruption to Santa Clara would not stop already-taped-out parts from shipping, but forward roadmap, software tuning, and supply allocation all center on this single campus.

nodes.name: Shin-Etsu Handotai Shirakawa
nodes.tagline: Half of the world's 300mm silicon wafers start as ingots pulled here.
nodes.summary: Shin-Etsu's Shirakawa site is the company's largest 300mm wafer operation, growing monocrystalline silicon ingots, slicing them to wafers, and polishing them for the logic, memory, and image-sensor industries.
nodes.body:
## The long story

A 300mm wafer begins as a single silicon seed crystal dipped into molten
polysilicon and slowly withdrawn, pulling a metre-long cylindrical boule of
perfect crystal. The boule is sliced, lapped, etched, and mirror-polished
before shipping to fabs.

## If this disappears

Capacity outside Shin-Etsu and SUMCO is thin — a few smaller Taiwanese,
Korean, and Chinese suppliers produce at far lower volumes and tighter
qualification scopes, few of them approved for the most demanding nodes.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: Shin-Etsu and SUMCO together supply the majority of the world's 300mm wafers. A prolonged outage at Shirakawa forces every leading-edge fab onto rationed supply within a quarter.

nodes.name: SK hynix M16
nodes.tagline: The lead producer of HBM — the memory every AI accelerator waits on.
nodes.summary: SK hynix's Icheon complex, anchored by the M16 fab, produces DRAM and the high-bandwidth memory (HBM) stacks that sit next to every current-generation AI accelerator.
nodes.body:
## The long story

HBM stacks up to twelve DRAM dies vertically with through-silicon vias,
then bonds the whole assembly to a logic base die before shipment to
packaging sites like TSMC for CoWoS integration with GPU logic dies.

## If this disappears

Samsung and Micron supply smaller volumes of HBM, but requalification for a
Nvidia or AMD platform takes quarters, and neither rival has matched
SK hynix's yield on the most advanced stacks.

keyFacts.label: Primary customer
keyFacts.value: Nvidia (sole HBM3E qualified supplier through most of 2024)

nodes.chokepointNarrative: SK hynix has held a clear lead in HBM3 and HBM3E with Nvidia's platforms designed around its supply. Samsung and Micron are closing in, but any prolonged Icheon disruption would constrain AI training roadmaps globally.

nodes.name: Spruce Pine quartz mine
nodes.tagline: A single Appalachian town supplies the purest quartz on Earth.
nodes.summary: The Spruce Pine pegmatite district yields high-purity quartz used to make the crucibles that hold molten silicon during ingot pulls. No comparable deposit is mined at scale anywhere else.
nodes.body:
## The long story

The pegmatite ore around Spruce Pine formed 380 million years ago under
conditions that left its quartz almost unbelievably pure. Sibelco and
competitors mill and chemically refine it into the crucible-grade powder that
nearly every silicon ingot in the world is pulled from.

## If this disappears

Lower-grade quartz exists elsewhere, but using it forces ingot manufacturers
to shorten pulls, lose yield, and replace crucibles more often — quietly
raising the cost of every wafer.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: The quartz crucibles made from Spruce Pine ore are single-use consumables, and every monocrystalline silicon ingot touches one. A prolonged shutdown — as happened briefly after Hurricane Helene in 2024 — threatens wafer supply across the entire industry within months.

nodes.name: Three Mile Island Unit 1
nodes.tagline: A closed nuclear reactor Microsoft is paying to restart for AI power.
nodes.summary: TMI Unit 1 operated safely for decades — it's the neighboring Unit 2 that partially melted down in 1979. Unit 1 shut for economic reasons in 2019 and is now slated to restart as a dedicated power source for Microsoft AI datacenters.
nodes.body:
## The long story

Constellation is relicensing Unit 1 with the NRC and rebuilding the
turbine, generator, and cooling systems. Microsoft has signed a long-term
power-purchase agreement to use the plant's output for datacenter
operations in the Mid-Atlantic region.

## If this disappears

The US has other reactivation candidates — Palisades in Michigan, potentially
Duane Arnold in Iowa — but no combination of those matches TMI's speed,
scale, or location near existing datacenter capacity.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: TMI is one of several nuclear reactivation and new-SMR deals. Any single project slipping is absorbable; the systemic risk sits in whether the wave of gigawatt-class AI sites can actually secure firm power on anything like its announced schedule.

nodes.name: TSMC Fab 21
nodes.tagline: The first leading-edge US fab capacity in more than a decade.
nodes.summary: TSMC's Phoenix campus builds out multiple phases of N4 and eventually N3/N2 logic — the US-based backup to Hsinchu's concentration risk, and the cornerstone of the CHIPS Act investment case.
nodes.body:
## The long story

Phase 1 was delayed by workforce and construction shortfalls before moving
into initial N4 production. Phase 2 and Phase 3 are planned to bring N3 and
eventually N2 online — contingent on continued CHIPS Act funding and
workforce availability.

## If this disappears

The industry loses redundancy it is only just beginning to build. Hsinchu
carries the load for leading-edge logic for several more years regardless.

keyFacts.label: Target process nodes
keyFacts.value: N4 initially, N3/N2 in later phases

nodes.chokepointNarrative: As of 2025 the site is still ramping, with initial phases commissioning but nowhere near Hsinchu volumes. Its strategic value is future-tense: a second source for leading-edge logic inside the US industrial base.

nodes.name: TSMC Fab 18
nodes.tagline: Where every leading-edge AI chip is actually made.
nodes.summary: TSMC's flagship leading-edge facility, running N5, N4, and N3 process nodes. Every Nvidia H100 and H200 passes through it.
nodes.body:
## The long story

Fab 18 is where abstract chip designs become physical silicon. Multiple phases
built out between 2018 and 2023 now run N5, N4, and N3 processes side by side,
fed by EUV scanners parked in adjacent clean rooms.

## If this disappears

No other facility can absorb the volume. Samsung's leading-edge yields still
trail, and Intel Foundry's 18A ramp is years from matching Fab 18's throughput.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: A disruption to Fab 18 halts production of nearly every leading-edge AI accelerator in the world. No substitute capacity comes online before 2026.

nodes.name: Wacker Burghausen
nodes.tagline: One of the few Western polysilicon producers at leading-edge purity.
nodes.summary: Wacker's Burghausen complex refines metallurgical silicon into electronic-grade polysilicon — the raw feedstock from which every silicon ingot is pulled.
nodes.body:
## The long story

Wacker's siemens-process reactors distill trichlorosilane gas onto heated rods
of ultra-pure silicon, slowly growing polysilicon bars that later get crushed
into chunks and shipped to wafer makers in Japan, Taiwan, and Korea.

## If this disappears

Fab-grade polysilicon has few substitutes. Ingot pullers could lean on Hemlock
in Michigan and a few smaller producers, but lead times on new capacity run to
years.
nodes.chokepointNarrative: Chinese producers dominate solar-grade polysilicon, but the stricter electronic-grade market is supplied by a handful of Western plants. Burghausen is the most important of them; its loss would force fabs to compete for a much shallower global pool.

flows.material: High-purity quartz for optics and crucibles
flows.notes: Reaches ASML via multiple intermediaries; shown direct here.
Each machine ships in pieces aboard chartered 747s.

flows.material: Quartz crucibles used to pull silicon ingots
HBM stacks for CoWoS integration with GPU dies
Packaged GPU modules for server integration

flows.notes: TMI Unit 1 restart targets 2028 commissioning for Microsoft.
